basics.name: functionAppName
basics.label: Function App Name
basics.toolTip: Globally unique name for the Azure Function App. The endpoint will be at https://<name>.azurewebsites.net/api/certificatesecretproxy. Allowed characters: letters, numbers, hyphens.

constraints.validationMessage: 2–60 characters. Must start and end with a letter or number. Only letters, numbers, and hyphens allowed.

basics.name: appServicePlanSku
basics.label: Hosting Plan
basics.toolTip: Y1 is the cheapest option — you pay only for invocations. Basic (B1–B3) plans run 24/7 on dedicated compute. Elastic Premium (EP1–EP3) adds pre-warmed instances and VNet integration.

allowedValues.label: B1 — Basic (1 core, 1.75 GB RAM, always-on)
B2 — Basic (2 cores, 3.5 GB RAM, always-on)
B3 — Basic (4 cores, 7 GB RAM, always-on)
EP1 — Elastic Premium (1 core, 3.5 GB RAM, pre-warmed)
EP2 — Elastic Premium (2 cores, 7 GB RAM, pre-warmed)
EP3 — Elastic Premium (4 cores, 14 GB RAM, pre-warmed)

subLabel.preValidation: Configure how client certificates are validated

options.text: At least one of the two validation methods below must be configured. Both can be active simultaneously — the certificate must then satisfy both checks. You can also leave both empty now and configure them after deployment.

elements.name: certRootThumbprint
elements.label: Root CA Certificate Thumbprint
elements.toolTip: Thumbprint of the Root CA certificate you will upload in the Azure Portal (Function App → Certificates → Public key certificates). When set, every client certificate must chain up to this CA. Recommended for managed device fleets — any device certificate issued by your corporate CA is automatically trusted.
elements.placeholder: Leave empty to skip chain validation

constraints.validationMessage: Must be a 40-character hexadecimal thumbprint, or leave empty.

elements.name: allowedClientCerts
elements.label: Allowed Client Certificate Thumbprints
elements.toolTip: Semicolon-separated list of client certificate thumbprints that are explicitly allowed (uppercase hex, no spaces). Example: 22E4D9050A50F3AC0A6588C641BD4BE869F788CD;AABBCC... Suitable for a small number of known devices.

options.text: After deployment you must upload your Root CA certificate in the Azure Portal (Function App → Certificates → Public key certificates) and set WEBSITE_LOAD_CERTIFICATES=* — the ARM template sets this automatically when certRootThumbprint is provided.

elements.name: workload
elements.label: Secret Backend
elements.toolTip: Controls where the function looks for secrets. App Settings: each secret is a Function App setting whose name matches the SecretName the client sends. Key Vault: secrets are fetched from Azure Key Vault using the system-assigned managed identity. Table Storage: secrets are rows in an Azure Table.

options.text: After deployment, add each secret as a Function App application setting. The setting name is exactly what device clients pass as 'SecretName'.

elements.name: keyVaultName
elements.label: Key Vault Name
elements.toolTip: Name of the existing Azure Key Vault that contains the secrets. After deployment, grant the Function App system-assigned managed identity the 'Key Vault Secrets User' role on the vault. The managed identity principal ID is shown in the deployment outputs.

constraints.validationMessage: 3–24 characters. Must start with a letter, end with a letter or number, and contain only letters, numbers, and hyphens.

options.text: After deployment, run: az role assignment create --assignee <principalId> --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' --scope <keyVaultResourceId>. The principal ID is available in the deployment outputs.

elements.name: tableEndpoint
elements.label: Table Storage Endpoint
elements.toolTip: Full Azure Table Storage URL including the table name. Format: https://<account>.table.core.windows.net/<TableName>. Table rows must have PartitionKey='secret', RowKey=<SecretName>. The function uses the managed identity to authenticate.

constraints.validationMessage: Must be a valid Azure Table Storage URL including the table name.
